_comment: Real seed nuggets from the 8 PoC videos, in the final format (hook + context + payload, binary worth_full_watch). Loaded into Supabase via gen_seed_sql.py -> MCP. interest_area: ai|build|str.

videos.title: 800+ hours of Learning Claude Code in 8 minutes

nuggets.hook: Define Claude Code sub-agents by TASK, not by ROLE.
nuggets.context: Sub-agents are separate Claude instances you spawn from your main session, each with its own context window and tools (create via /agents).
nuggets.payload: Assigning sub-agents as personas — 'front-end dev', 'PM', 'UX designer' — produced noticeably worse results than plain Claude. They shine when scoped to a concrete TASK instead: cleaning up code just written, generating docs, or gathering web research. Define them by the job, not the job title.

nuggets.hook: Build a sub-agent that reviews your own UI in a real browser.
nuggets.context: Playwright MCP lets an agent drive a browser and inspect the live DOM.
nuggets.payload: Wire a sub-agent to the Playwright MCP so it opens your app, inspects the rendered components, and returns design/usability feedback. It catches UI issues the main agent can't see, and it does the work in its own context window so it never pollutes your main session's tokens.

nuggets.hook: Add 'use Context7' to a prompt to pull live, current docs.
nuggets.context: Context7 is an MCP server that fetches up-to-date documentation for popular libraries.
nuggets.payload: Models go stale on fast-moving frameworks. Instead of Googling and pasting docs, install the Context7 MCP and just write 'use Context7' in your prompt — Claude fetches the latest docs for that library automatically before writing code. Kills a whole class of wrong-API-version bugs.

nuggets.hook: Turn your repeated prompts into a custom slash-command library.
nuggets.context: Claude Code reads markdown command files from a .claude/commands/ folder.
nuggets.payload: Any prompt you type more than twice — 'add an API endpoint with error handling and types', 'lint and fix the whole codebase' — should become a reusable command. Drop it in .claude/commands/*.md, give it arguments for flexibility, and organize into subfolders as it grows. You stop re-typing and your team inherits the same shortcuts.

nuggets.hook: A solo-dev MCP stack: Supabase, Playwright, Stripe, Vercel.
nuggets.context: MCP servers connect Claude to external tools and services.
nuggets.payload: Concretely: Supabase MCP (query the DB, apply migrations, create tables), Chrome-DevTools/Playwright (autonomous front-end debugging in a real browser), Stripe (anything touching payments), Vercel (project settings + latest docs). Connecting these turns Claude from a code typist into something that can operate your whole stack.

nuggets.hook: Press # to write a standing instruction to Claude's memory.
nuggets.context: Claude Code stores persistent instructions in a CLAUDE.md file.
nuggets.payload: Anything you keep re-explaining each session should be saved once: press # and add the instruction, choosing project-local or global. It writes to CLAUDE.md so you can edit/remove later. Stops you repeating the same context every time you open a session.

nuggets.hook: Review code in a FRESH session before shipping, not the one that wrote it.
nuggets.payload: Before pushing to production, open a new Claude session and ask it to review the files you just touched for security, performance, and error handling. The building session is biased toward its own work and accumulated context; a clean session catches what it glossed over.

videos.title: Claude Code NEW Sub Agents in 7 Minutes

nuggets.hook: Give each Claude Code sub-agent its own scoped tools — not the full kit.
nuggets.context: Sub-agents are separate Claude instances saved as markdown in .claude/agents/, each with its own context window, system prompt, and tool permissions.
nuggets.payload: When you create one, grant only the tools its job needs — a 'backend' agent gets database read + SQL + log access; a 'frontend' agent gets the browser/Playwright tools and nothing else. Scoping keeps each agent's context clean, lowers the risk it does something destructive outside its lane, and produces sharper output than one over-permissioned agent juggling everything.

nuggets.hook: Encode your taste as permanent rules in a sub-agent's system prompt.
nuggets.context: Each sub-agent has its own system prompt (the body of its markdown file) that it reads on every run.
nuggets.payload: Write your recurring stylistic gripes into it once — e.g. 'never use linear gradients, thick fonts, or emojis' for a UI agent — and it stops making those choices every time, instead of you re-correcting them each session. Treat the system prompt as an accumulating style guide for that agent.

nuggets.hook: Your sub-agents are just markdown files — commit them so the setup is shareable.
nuggets.context: Each agent lives as a plain .md file in .claude/agents/ in your repo.
nuggets.payload: Because they're just files, you can commit them to git and reuse them across machines and teammates — anyone who clones the repo gets your specialized agents automatically. A good agent you build once becomes part of the project's tooling instead of living only on your laptop.

nuggets.hook: Sub-agents aren't just for code — wire them to Gmail, Linear, or Canva.
nuggets.context: Sub-agents can use any MCP server you've connected, not only coding tools.
nuggets.payload: Give an agent a non-coding MCP (Gmail to triage email, Linear to file tickets, Canva for assets) and it becomes a scoped specialist for that workflow — a 'mini workforce' where each agent owns one job with exactly the tools it needs. Useful once you stop thinking of agents as code-only.

nuggets.hook: The single line between an AI 'workflow' and an AI 'agent'.
nuggets.payload: A workflow is a path a HUMAN pre-defines: 'first check the calendar, then fetch weather, then speak the answer.' It becomes an AGENT the moment the LLM itself becomes the decision-maker — choosing what to do, doing it via tools, observing the result, and deciding whether to iterate. If a human wrote the steps, it's a workflow no matter how many steps; if the model picks the steps, it's an agent.

nuggets.hook: Make an agent self-improve by adding a second LLM as a critic.
nuggets.payload: Instead of accepting the first draft, have the agent pass its output to another LLM that critiques it against explicit criteria (e.g. 'LinkedIn best practices'), then revise and re-check in a loop until the criteria are met. This is how an agent does autonomously what you'd otherwise do by hand re-prompting.

nuggets.hook: RAG is just a type of AI workflow — nothing exotic.
nuggets.context: RAG = retrieval-augmented generation, a term thrown around a lot.
nuggets.payload: Strip the jargon: RAG is simply a process that makes the model look something up (your calendar, a weather API, a doc store) before it answers. It's a pre-defined retrieval step in a workflow — that's the whole idea.

nuggets.hook: ReAct = Reason + Act, the default shape of an agent.
nuggets.payload: The most common agent configuration is ReAct: the model reasons about what to do, acts via a tool, observes the result, and repeats. When people say 'agent', this reason-act loop is usually what's running under the hood.

videos.title: How to Get Your First Customers | Startup School

nuggets.hook: The cold sales-email formula that actually converts.
nuggets.payload: Keep it to 6-8 sentences, plain text (no HTML — write like you would to a friend), no jargon. Name the customer's specific problem, say you're the founder, include show-don't-tell social proof, link a simple site (screenshots + bullets, not graphics), and end with ONE clear ask. Long, formatted, jargon-y emails get archived.

nuggets.hook: In B2B, never offer a free trial — use a money-back guarantee instead.
nuggets.payload: Free trials train customers to not pay. Charge upfront with a 30/60-day money-back guarantee plus a monthly opt-out. If they won't pay at all, that's your signal to move on, not to discount. Keep raising price until customers complain but still pay — that's the right price.

nuggets.hook: Outbound is a numbers game because most people aren't early adopters.
nuggets.payload: When someone ignores your email, it's almost never because they hate your product — they just archive everything and never try new tools. You can't convert a non-adopter, so don't try; send more outreach to FIND the few early adopters who love trying new things. Volume is how you locate them.

nuggets.hook: Work backwards from your goal through the funnel drop-offs.
nuggets.payload: Real numbers: 500 emails → 50% open → 5% reply (20) → 50% take a demo (10) → 2 close. To land 10 customers you need FAR more than 10 leads, and you must track each conversion step in a CRM. Without the data you can't tell whether you're bad at emailing or bad at closing.

nuggets.hook: Your first customers should be your EASIEST, not your hardest.
nuggets.payload: Don't chase the prestigious logo or the complex deal early. Pick the leads most likely to close and make the sales process as easy on yourself as possible. You don't have time to chase every lead — prioritize the ones already leaning yes.

nuggets.hook: Sell to startups first — especially if you sell software.
nuggets.payload: Big companies have procurement, departments whose job is to slow you down, and long decision chains. Startups have none of that: short decision lines, you reach the actual decision-maker in your first email, and they move fast. That's why early-stage software companies should target other startups.

nuggets.hook: The #1 founder mistake: declaring 'sales doesn't work' after too few emails.
nuggets.payload: Send 100 emails at normal conversion rates and you get ~0 customers — then founders quit sales for 'marketing' or 'SEO'. But 100 emails is far too little data to conclude anything. You didn't reach enough early adopters yet; the answer is more outreach, not a different channel.

nuggets.hook: Manually recruit your first customers — do things that don't scale.
nuggets.payload: Brex onboarded its first 10 customers by hand from its own YC batch. The scalable channels (SEO, SEM, referrals) are END STATES, not how you start. Early on, you personally recruit each customer; the un-scalable manual work is the point.

nuggets.hook: Founders must do sales themselves before hiring anyone.
nuggets.payload: Don't hire a sales team until you personally know what 'good' looks like — otherwise you can't tell if a hire is doing it right. Sales and talking-to-users are the same coin: you can't outsource learning your customer.

nuggets.hook: Don't be afraid to let a customer go.
nuggets.payload: If a prospect drags you through 2-3 calls without moving, it's fine to say 'this has been great, let's reconnect in six months' and move on. Chasing slow leads burns the time you should spend finding fast ones.

videos.title: The One-Person Business Model (How To Productize Yourself)

nuggets.hook: Be a generalist, not a specialist — specialists get automated.
nuggets.payload: If your work is one repeatable skill performed the same way each time, it's the easiest thing to automate or outsource — 'if you work like a robot, you'll be replaced by one.' The durable position is the generalist who moves across a domain and hires/directs specialists (including AI) rather than being one.

nuggets.hook: The 'domain of mastery' system for never running out of content.
nuggets.payload: Pick three interests — one that makes money, one that genuinely excites you, and one development-based (psychology/philosophy). Generate all your content from the intersection: their principles, sub-topics, the mentors you learn from, and the connections between them. The three-way blend is what makes your perspective unique instead of generic.

nuggets.hook: Start monetizing immediately with a Minimum Viable Offer.
nuggets.payload: Don't wait for a polished product. Sell either (a) a single freelance skill for $500-1k, or (b) a single-interest consulting service — a pack of 4 calls for $500-1k. The point is to start charging and iterating now; your first offer will be rough and that's exactly why you must ship it.

nuggets.hook: Sell consulting/teaching, not done-for-you work — it scales better.
nuggets.payload: Instead of doing the work for clients (freelance), sell 4 calls teaching them to do X — web design, cold email, ads. It's more scalable than delivery work, and many buyers actually prefer being taught. Surprisingly under-offered: digital tutoring on a concrete skill.

nuggets.hook: Map your business with four pillars.
nuggets.payload: goals → your brand (what you're leading people toward); problems in the way → your content (what you help them overcome); systems that solve them → your product (how they solve it faster); benefits to your own life → your marketing (why they should care). Each pillar of the business derives from one part of your own journey.

nuggets.hook: Use the consulting calls to build a product with proof baked in.
nuggets.payload: Run the MVO consulting calls, note the questions and patterns that repeat across them, then package those into a course/cohort. You arrive at the product with real results and testimonials already in hand, and you can shift off 1:1 client work as your audience grows.

videos.title: My 4 Most Powerful STR Pricing Tricks

nuggets.hook: PriceLabs has a 'range of efficacy' — beat it at the edges.
nuggets.context: PriceLabs is the dominant Airbnb dynamic-pricing tool; ~70% of hosts use it or similar.
nuggets.payload: Because most hosts run the same software, everyone's prices herd up and down together. PriceLabs is only accurate in a band — say 60-84% occupancy in your market. ABOVE that band it under-raises (you leave money on the table); BELOW it, it won't cut enough to actually get you booked. Override it manually at both extremes.

nuggets.hook: Set minimum-length-of-stay from the length of the high-demand run.
nuggets.context: PriceLabs shows a color-coded demand calendar (dark blue = highest demand).
nuggets.payload: Match your minimum-stay to the dark-blue run: a 5-day dark-blue stretch → 4-day MLS; a 2-day stretch → 3-day MLS; 7+ days → investigate why, maybe 5-day MLS. The logic: there are two genuinely popular days and people extend around them — you want to capture the longer-stay guest inside that run.

nuggets.hook: Airbnb 'rule sets' are your most powerful lever — and unique to Airbnb.
nuggets.context: Rule sets let you override your global pricing on specific days/conditions.
nuggets.payload: Airbnb supports day-by-day 3-day, 4-day, early-bird, and last-minute discounts that other platforms (Vrbo) simply don't have. You can keep a 10% global weekly discount and override it one day at a time. If you're Airbnb-primary, rule sets are the highest-leverage setting most hosts ignore.

nuggets.hook: The 'drive-down' rule set auto-cuts weekday prices you'd forget to drop.
nuggets.payload: Stack a 3-day length-of-stay discount WITH a last-minute discount on the same day (Airbnb allows both at once). It automatically drives down the price of soft weekday inventory as the date approaches — so you don't lose bookings just because you forgot to manually discount.

nuggets.hook: Feed your payout CSV to AI — but split it by property TYPE first.
nuggets.payload: Export your past payouts as a CSV and give it to ChatGPT/Claude, but split it: all beach houses in one file, all apartments in another, so it compares apples to apples. It surfaces per-weekday occupancy you'd miss — an 85% overall occupancy can hide a 2% Monday that you can then fix with pricing.

nuggets.hook: Weekdays are only worth as much as the weekends they're attached to.
nuggets.payload: The core physics of STR pricing: a Wednesday's value comes from the booked weekend next to it. If a Thursday-Saturday books but the following Mon-Wed sits open, that isolated midweek inventory is no longer worth what the software thinks — price it accordingly.

nuggets.hook: Forecast future occupancy from the PriceLabs occupancy chart.
nuggets.payload: For a future date, compare last-year-today vs this-year-today occupancy, multiply the ratio by last year's FINAL occupancy, then adjust by current booking velocity/pickup. It lets you project where you'll land 3-6 months out and decide now whether to be aggressive or generous on price.

nuggets.hook: In peak season, push BOTH occupancy and ADR via restrictions.
nuggets.payload: Normally you trade occupancy against rate. Peak season is the exception — demand is high enough that you hold the power, so you can raise rates AND tighten minimum-stay restrictions at the same time without losing bookings. Don't leave that money on the table.

videos.title: The (Overdue) Collapse Of Short Term Rentals

nuggets.hook: STR demand is price-driven, not experience-driven — and Airbnb hides it.
nuggets.payload: A study found 61% chose Airbnb as a BUDGET alternative even to budget hotels, and 70% booked a whole place specifically to avoid sharing with a host. Yet Airbnb spends billions marketing 'stay with a local host' — because shared-stay listings are far harder for cities to regulate than whole-home investor listings. The folksy image is legal cover.

nuggets.hook: The STR margin-compression thesis.
nuggets.payload: As STRs pushed long-term rents up and the market oversaturated, the gap between STR yield and long-term-rental yield narrowed. For many hosts the extra work and risk no longer pays — and leveraged hosts who only qualified for their loans using projected STR income may be forced to sell. Watch the yield gap, not the headline ADR.

nuggets.hook: Regulation is the real structural threat to STR, not demand.
nuggets.payload: Cities are capping or banning STRs, forcing platforms to delist unregistered units, and HOAs are blocking short stays. ~45% of LA listings were non-compliant — many operators just treat fines as a cost of doing business, but that's a fragile model when the legislation tightens.

nuggets.hook: Airbnb has no real moat besides its brand.
nuggets.payload: Booking.com, Expedia, and TripAdvisor are all launching the same whole-home rental product, and hosts increasingly list through local agencies and split the ~17% fee savings with guests. There's little stopping a competitor from taking share on price or service — useful context if you're betting on the platform.

nuggets.hook: The low-effort 'Airbnb Baron' operating kit.
nuggets.payload: Investor-operators standardized on: remote-changeable keypad locks (no key handoff between guests), easily-cleanable surfaces, cheap-but-fashionable fittings, and properties with minimal landscaping. Every choice is designed to cut the per-turnover effort so one person can run many units.

videos.title: How To Build A $10M AI App In Literally 30 Seconds

nuggets.hook: The repeatable AI-app playbook: inefficient industry + GPT wrapper, run 100x.
nuggets.context: Amjad Masad is the CEO of Replit.
nuggets.payload: Go into an inefficient industry you actually understand, build a focused AI tool that automates its most painful manual work, and do it many times — one will take off. Magic School did it for teachers; Synthesis for tutoring. Domain understanding + a narrow AI wrapper is the pattern, not a grand platform.

nuggets.hook: Distribution is now the only moat — building is commoditized.
nuggets.payload: When anyone can build the app, the hard part becomes getting customers. If you have an audience ('you can get on a microphone and talk about it'), that distribution IS your edge — more than technical skill, which used to be the barrier and no longer is. The advantage shifted from who can build to who can get attention.

nuggets.hook: Software's cost just collapsed ~4000x.
nuggets.payload: Apps that would have cost ~$100k in developer time can now be built for ~$25 of agent usage on a tool like Replit. The constraint on building software is no longer money or engineers — it's knowing what's worth building. Internalize the magnitude of that shift.

nuggets.hook: AI revenue ramps broke the historical frame.
nuggets.payload: Reaching $10M ARR in 3-4 months is now happening; Jasper hit ~$50M ARR in ~10 months. These curves 'didn't exist' in the web-2.0 era. It raises a real question — is it sustainable? — but it means the speed of validation (and of competition) is now radically faster than your instincts expect.

nuggets.hook: 'Shopify for software' — the mental model for the AI app wave.
nuggets.payload: Just as Shopify + Alibaba let people build product brands without ever manufacturing (one host ran a $50M e-commerce brand without making a product or building a site), AI agents let non-programmers be 'software creators.' You skip the hard craft and go straight to the part that matters: the idea and the customers.

nuggets.hook: Magic School: a teacher's AI tool to ~4M users in ~1 year.
nuggets.payload: A teacher learned to code on Replit during COVID and built an AI tool that generates lesson plans and quizzes. It hit ~4M educators and ~$20M raised in about a year — selling into schools, normally the HARDEST market, worked precisely because it removed 4 hours/night of grunt work. Solve a painful, time-eating chore for an underserved group.

nuggets.hook: The GPT-wrapper moat question — and the honest answer.
nuggets.payload: Wrappers are easy to clone, which means price competition and the foundation-model company (OpenAI/Anthropic) capturing most of the value. The honest answer: moats only form over TIME through accumulated infrastructure, scale, and switching costs — not on day one. Don't assume early ARR means a defensible business.

nuggets.hook: Solve a problem you feel 10x more than everyone else.
nuggets.payload: Amjad felt the pain of setting up a coding environment acutely because he coded in a Jordan internet cafe, re-setting up every single visit. That outsized, daily pain is why he saw the '$100 bill on the ground' that thousands of programmers walked past. Look for problems your specific life makes unavoidable.

nuggets.hook: Naval's only networking rule: do something great and the network appears.
nuggets.payload: He didn't try to get coffee with Brendan Eich (inventor of JavaScript) — he built something so cool that Eich reached out to HIM. The leverage isn't in outreach; it's in producing work good enough that the people you want to meet come find you.

nuggets.hook: Public writing compounds into opportunity.
nuggets.payload: Amjad wrote articles about the hard problems Replit was solving; they kept hitting Hacker News; Paul Graham (who reads HN) noticed — and that's how he got into YC after FOUR rejections. Writing in public about your real work is a slow-burn distribution and credibility engine.

nuggets.hook: Kill the learning curve — drive time-to-value toward zero.
nuggets.payload: Amjad scrapped Replit's '100 days of code' onboarding with a blunt principle: 'no successful company requires 100 days to learn it.' He rebuilt the product to be ChatGPT-like — just a prompt. If your product needs a long ramp before value, that ramp is the thing to attack.

nuggets.hook: Build on top of an improving substrate — ride two exponentials.
nuggets.payload: Replit gets better from its own engineering AND from foundation models improving underneath it — every model upgrade improves the product for free. Position your product so someone else's exponential progress (the models) automatically lifts you, instead of fighting against it.

nuggets.hook: 'Go the distance' is an underrated founder edge — the Jobs/Pixar story.
nuggets.payload: Steve Jobs ran NeXT and Pixar at a loss for ~10 years, cutting personal checks to make payroll. Pixar became a $7B company and NeXT's operating system literally became Mac OS and saved Apple. Endurance through a long dead-zone is a real, undervalued advantage.

nuggets.hook: Ramen profitability changes the whole conversation.
nuggets.payload: Replit getting to ~$10k/month from educators and API users was enough to sustain the team — and it changed how investors (and YC) treated them. Modest real revenue that proves people will pay beats a polished deck with none.
